─[ Lore Diagnostics and Tool Compatibility ]─ ┐

Lore Diagnostics and Tool Compatibility

Three related ideas around runtime awareness, self-testing, and cross-tool compatibility tracking.

1. Version Awareness (/lore-version)

Users should be able to ask their instance what version of Lore they’re running.

Mechanism:

  • sync-framework.sh stamps framework.version (tag or SHA) into .lore-config at sync time
  • Every level in the chain carries the version it last synced from
  • A /lore-version command (or skill) reads .lore-config and reports:
    • Framework version (e.g., v0.1.0)
    • Last sync timestamp
    • Current level (org/team/instance/standalone)
    • Parent URL and pin (if applicable)

.lore-config addition:

framework:
  source_repo: https://github.com/lorehq/lore.git
  version: v0.1.0          # stamped by sync-framework.sh
  synced_at: 2026-02-12    # when last synced

Bonus: Could warn if the local version is behind source (stale sync detection).

2. Integration Health Check (/lore-check)

A diagnostic command that verifies Lore is properly integrated with the current tool (Claude Code, OpenCode, etc.).

What it would test:

  • Hooks are registered and firing (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse)
  • Skills are discoverable (registry matches skill dirs)
  • MkDocs builds cleanly (if applicable)
  • .lore-config is valid
  • Framework manifest matches expected files
  • Agents and registries are in sync

Difference from validate-consistency.sh:

  • validate-consistency.sh checks file/registry consistency (already exists)
  • /lore-check would be broader — also tests runtime integration (are hooks actually firing? can the agent read skills? does the tool support all expected hook events?)

Hook smoke test idea:

  • /lore-check triggers each hook type and checks for expected output
  • Reports which hooks fired vs which were silent (tool may not support all events)

3. Tool Compatibility Matrix

Different AI coding tools support different hook events, permission models, and capabilities. Lore should track what works where and how well.

Proposed matrix:

CapabilityClaude CodeOpenCode[Future Tool]
SessionStart hook??
UserPromptSubmit hook??
PreToolUse hook??
PostToolUse hook??
PostToolUseFailure hook??
Hook matcher filtering??
Skill discovery??
Agent delegation (Task)??
Permission profiles??
MCP tool integration??

Effectiveness scoring (0-10):

Not just “does it work” but “how well does it nudge/enforce”:

LevelScoreMeaning
0No supportTool doesn’t support this capability at all
1-3WeakFires but easily ignored, no enforcement
4-6ModerateReliable nudge, agent usually follows
7-9StrongConsistent enforcement, hard to bypass
10AbsoluteBlocking — agent cannot proceed without compliance

Example scoring:

  • Claude Code PostToolUse hook → maybe 4/10 (fires reliably but agent can ignore the reminder)
  • Claude Code PreToolUse hook with block → maybe 9/10 (actually prevents the action)
  • OpenCode permission deny → 10/10 (hard block)

Where this lives:

  • docs/operations/tool-compatibility.md in source — framework-level reference
  • Updated when new tools are added or existing tool behavior changes
  • /lore-check could auto-populate scores based on runtime testing

Open Questions

  1. Version propagation depth: Does every level stamp its own version, or just pass through the source version?
  2. Staleness threshold: How old is “too old” for a sync? 30 days? Configurable?
  3. Hook testing: Can we actually test hooks programmatically, or do we need the agent to self-report?
  4. Scoring methodology: Should effectiveness scores be subjective (maintainer judgment) or measured (automated compliance rate)?
  5. Tool-specific adapters: If a tool doesn’t support a hook type, should Lore try to emulate it through other means?
  6. Breaking changes: When a tool updates and loses functionality, how do we detect and alert?

Priority Assessment

  • /lore-version: Low effort, high value — implement soon (sync script change + new .lore-config field)
  • Tool compatibility matrix: Medium effort, high reference value — document what we already know
  • /lore-check: Higher effort, valuable for onboarding and debugging — plan after matrix exists
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
